text: \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{3\cdot7}+\frac{1}{7\cdot11}+\frac{1}{11\cdot15}+\cdots\) (\(n\) terms) \(=\)
explanation:
Using partial fractions: \(\dfrac{1}{(4k-1)(4k+3)}=\dfrac{1}{4}\left(\dfrac{1}{4k-1}-\dfrac{1}{4k+3}\right)\)

Telescoping sum:

\[\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4n+3}\right)=\frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{12}\]
name: Telescoping Series Limit
subject: maths

text: The quadrilateral formed by the pairs of lines \(xy+x+y+1=0\) and \(xy+3x+3y+9=0\) is
explanation:
Factor the first: \(xy+x+y+1=(x+1)(y+1)=0\), giving \(x=-1\) and \(y=-1\).

Factor the second: \(xy+3x+3y+9=(x+3)(y+3)=0\), giving \(x=-3\) and \(y=-3\).

The four lines \(x=-1\), \(y=-1\), \(x=-3\), \(y=-3\) form a quadrilateral. Since adjacent sides are perpendicular (\(x=\text{const}\) and \(y=\text{const}\)) and all sides are equal in length (\(|-3-(-1)|=2\)), it is a **square**.
name: Shape Formed by Two Pairs
subject: maths

text:
Identify the correct statements from the following:

I. Tendency to form halide hydrates gradually increases from Be to Ba down the group.
II. Tendency to form stable superoxides increases from Li to Cs down the group.
III. Low solubility of LiF is due to its high lattice energy.
IV. Solubility of carbonates of group-2 elements increases down the group.
explanation:
**Statement I — Incorrect:** The tendency to form halide hydrates actually **decreases** from Be to Ba, because Be and Mg have higher charge density and polarise water molecules more easily.

**Statement II — Correct:** Going down Group 1, cation size increases, which stabilises larger polyatomic anions like superoxide (\(\text{O}_2^-\)). The superoxide ion is large and polarisable, better stabilised by larger cations. So \(\text{KO}_2\), \(\text{RbO}_2\), \(\text{CsO}_2\) are stable; LiO₂ is not stable.

**Statement III — Correct:** LiF has a very **high lattice energy** due to the small size of both Li⁺ and F⁻ ions. The hydration enthalpy is insufficient to overcome this high lattice energy, resulting in very low solubility.

**Statement IV — Incorrect:** Solubility of Group 2 carbonates **decreases** down the group (BeCO₃ > MgCO₃ > CaCO₃ > SrCO₃ > BaCO₃).

Correct statements: **II and III**.
name: Questions Item 18
subject: che

text: Identify the pair of species having the **same hybridisation** for the central atom but possessing **different geometry**.
explanation:
We need the same hybridisation but different shape (due to lone pairs).

- **(a) CH₄ and NH₄⁺**: both sp³, both tetrahedral → same shape.
- **(b) C₂H₂ and BeCl₂**: both sp, both linear → same shape.
- **(c) PF₅ (sp³d, trigonal bipyramidal) and IF₅ (sp³d², square pyramidal)**: different hybridisations.
- **(d) PCl₅ and ClF₃**:
  - PCl₅: **sp³d**, trigonal bipyramidal (5 BP, 0 LP)
  - ClF₃: **sp³d**, T-shaped (3 BP, 2 LP)

Both have **sp³d hybridisation** but different molecular geometries due to different numbers of lone pairs. ✓
name: Same Hybridisation but Different Geometry
subject: che

text: The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by \(x = 0.5 \sin (100 t + \phi)\). The maximum velocity of the particle is
explanation: Velocity \(v = dx/dt = 50 \cos (100 t + \phi)\). Maximum velocity = 50 ms^{-1}.

text: Compound 'A' undergoes formation of cyanohydrin, which on hydrolysis gives lactic acid (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}\)). Therefore, compound 'A' is
explanation:
Lactic acid is \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH(OH)—COOH}\). Working backwards from lactic acid:

**Step 2 — Hydrolysis:** cyanohydrin \(\text{CH}_3\text{—CH(OH)—CN} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{CH}_3\text{—CH(OH)—COOH}\) (lactic acid)

**Step 1 — Cyanohydrin formation:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{HCN} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{—CH(OH)—CN}\)

So compound A must be **acetaldehyde** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}\)), which has a CH₃ group and a CHO group.
name: Questions Item 5
subject: che

text: Three point charges of 2 mC each are kept at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side 50 cm. If the system is supplied energy at the rate of 2 kW, the time taken to move one of the charges to the mid point of the line joining the other two charges is
explanation:
According to the question,

Energy of system of three charges,

\( U = \left( \frac{kq_1 q_2}{r} \right)^3 \)

or \( U = \frac{k(2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3})}{(1/2)} \times 3 = 24 k \times 10^{-6} \text{J} \)

When one charge move to the mid point of the line joining the other two charges.

Energy of the system,

\( U' = \frac{k(2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6})}{(1/4)} \times 2 + \frac{k(2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6})}{1/2} \)

\( U' = 40 k \times 10^{-6} \text{J} \)

Net energy, \( \Delta U = U' - U = (40 - 24) k \times 10^{-6} \)

or \( \Delta U = W = 16 \times 9 \times 10^{-3} \text{J} \)

Given that energy supplied to the system at the rate of 2 kW.

So, \( W = 2 \text{kW} \times t = 16 \times 9 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times t \)

\( \Rightarrow t = 72 \text{s} \).
name: Questions Item 23
subject: phy

text: \(\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}[\sqrt{x^2+ax+b}-x]\) (\(a<0<b\)) depends on
explanation:
Rationalising:

\[\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{ax+b}{\sqrt{x^2+ax+b}+x}=\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{a+b/x}{\sqrt{1+a/x+b/x^2}+1}=\frac{a}{2}\]

The limit depends **only on \(a\)**.
name: Limit of Square Root Expression
subject: maths

text: If \(f(x)=x^{1/x}\) for \(x>0\), then \(f\) is
explanation:
\(\dfrac{d}{dx}x^{1/x}=x^{1/x}\cdot\dfrac{1-\ln x}{x^2}\)

Positive when \(1-\ln x>0\), i.e., \(x<e\). Negative when \(x>e\).

Increasing on \((1,e)\) and decreasing on \((e,\infty)\).

subject: maths

text: If \(s=5\sin(2t)\), the velocity at \(t=\dfrac{\pi}{3}\) s is
explanation: \(v=\dfrac{ds}{dt}=10\cos2t\bigg|_{t=\pi/3}=10\cos\dfrac{2\pi}{3}=10\cdot\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=-5\)

text: The angular deviation of 5th order dark fringe is 12° in a single slit experiment. If the width of the slit is 9 µm then the wavelength of the incident light is
explanation: \( \theta = n \frac{\lambda}{d} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{12 \times \pi}{180} \times \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{5} = 3768 \) Å.

text: For real number \(x\), if the minimum value of \(f(x) = x^2+2bx+2c^2\) is greater than the maximum value of \(g(x) = -x^2-2cx+b^2\), then
explanation: Minimum of \(f(x) = x^2+2bx+2c^2 = (x+b)^2+2c^2-b^2\), so min value \(= 2c^2-b^2\). Rewrite \(g(x) = -(x+c)^2+b^2+c^2\), so max value \(= b^2+c^2\). Condition: \(2c^2-b^2 > b^2+c^2 \Rightarrow c^2 > 2b^2\).

subject: maths

text: If the enthalpy of combustion of carbon to CO₂(g) is \(-394.0\) kJ mol⁻¹, the enthalpy change for the formation of 17.6 g of CO₂ from carbon and dioxygen at the same temperature is:
explanation:
Combustion reaction:
\[\text{C}(s) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g), \quad \Delta H = -394.0 \text{ kJ/mol}\]

Molar mass of CO₂ = 44 g/mol

Moles of CO₂ in 17.6 g = \(\frac{17.6}{44} = 0.4\) mol

\[\Delta H = -394.0 \times 0.4 = -157.6 \text{ kJ}\]
name: Enthalpy of CO₂ Formation for Partial Amount
subject: che

text: Six point charges each of magnitude Q are placed at the vertices of a regular hexagon of side a. Electric field intensity on the line passing through the centre O and perpendicular to the plane at large distance x (>> a) from O is
explanation: Net field = 4 k Q a / x³.

text: If the kinetic energy of O₂ gas is 4.0 kJ mol⁻¹, its RMS speed in cm s⁻¹ is:
explanation:
Given: KE = 4000 J/mol, \(M = 32 \times 10^{-3}\) kg/mol

\[v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times KE}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4000}{32 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{250000} = 500 \text{ m/s}\]

Converting: \(500 \text{ m/s} = 500 \times 100 = 5.0 \times 10^4 \text{ cm/s}\)
name: RMS Speed from Kinetic Energy
subject: che

explanation: Same as the previous: \(\int_0^1\sqrt{x}\,dx=\dfrac{2}{3}\)

text: Unpolarised light from air incidents on the surface of a transparent medium of refractive index 1.414 such that the reflected light is completely polarised. Match the angles given in List-I with the corresponding values given in List-II.
explanation:
Polarising angle \( i_p = \sin^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \).

Refraction angle \( r = \sin^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \).

Deviation \( \delta = \sin^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} - \sin^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \).

Angle of reflection \( \cos^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \).

Angle between incident and polarised \( 2 \sin^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \).

subject: phy

text: Which among the following is most common in alkyl halides?
explanation:
Alkyl halides have a polar C—X bond (\(\overset{\delta+}{\text{C}}\text{—}\overset{\delta-}{\text{X}}\)). The carbon is electron-deficient and susceptible to attack by **nucleophiles** (electron-rich species). Alkyl halides readily undergo **nucleophilic substitution** reactions:

- **\(S_N2\)**: Bimolecular, concerted backside attack by nucleophile
- **\(S_N1\)**: Unimolecular, via carbocation intermediate

\[\overset{\delta+}{\text{R}}\text{—}\overset{\delta-}{\text{X}} + \text{Nu}^\ominus \rightarrow \text{R—Nu} + \text{X}^-\]
name: Questions Item 1
subject: che

text: For a thermodynamic system, which among the following is NOT a state function?
explanation:
A **state function** depends only on the current state of the system (initial and final), not on the path taken.

- **Enthalpy (H)**, **Internal energy (U)**, and **Gibbs free energy (G)** are all state functions.
- **Work (W)** is a **path function** — its value depends on how the process is carried out.

For example, the work done in a reversible expansion is different from that in an irreversible expansion between the same initial and final states.
name: State Functions vs Path Functions
subject: che

text: The equation of the circle whose radius is \(3\) and which touches internally the circle \(x^2+y^2-4x-6y-12=0\) at \((-1,-1)\) is
explanation:
Given circle has centre \(C_1=(2,3)\), \(r_1=5\). Required circle has \(r_2=3\) and touches internally at \(A(-1,-1)\).

For internal tangency, \(A\) divides \(C_1C_2\) externally in \(r_1:r_2=5:3\):

\[(-1,-1)=\frac{5C_2-3C_1}{5-3}\]

\[C_2=\left(\frac{4}{5},\frac{7}{5}\right)\]

Equation: \(\left(x-\dfrac{4}{5}\right)^2+\left(y-\dfrac{7}{5}\right)^2=9\)

Multiplying through: \(5x^2+5y^2-8x-14y-32=0\).
name: Internal Tangency Circle
subject: maths

text: The coordinates of the focus of the parabola \((x+3)^2=2(y-5)\) is
explanation:
In the form \((x-h)^2=4a(y-k)\): vertex \((-3,5)\), \(4a=2 \implies a=\dfrac{1}{2}\).

Parabola opens upward, focus at \(\left(-3, 5+\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=\left(-3,\dfrac{11}{2}\right)\).
name: Focus of Translated Parabola
subject: maths

text: The minimum and maximum values of \(\cos\!\left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) + 2\sqrt{2}\sin\!\left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\) are respectively
explanation:
Let \(f(x) = \cos\!\left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) + 2\sqrt{2}\sin\!\left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\).

Expanding using angle addition:
\[f(x) = a\cos x + b\sin x\]
where \(a = \dfrac{1}{2} + \sqrt{6}\) and \(b = 2 - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\).

\[a^2 + b^2 = \left(\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{6}\right)^2 + \left(2-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{4} + \sqrt{6} + 6 + 4 - 2\sqrt{3} + \frac{3}{4} = 9\]

So the maximum value is \(\sqrt{9} = 3\) and the minimum is \(-3\).
name: Max-Min of Trig Expression
subject: maths

text: Let \(P(n): 1^2+2^2+3^2+\cdots+n^2 = \dfrac{6(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-2020)+2n^3+3n^2+n}{6}\) for all \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). Then which of the following is correct?
explanation: We know \(1^2+2^2+\cdots+n^2 = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}\). The given RHS \(= (n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-2020)+\dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}\). For the two sides to be equal, the product \((n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-2020)\) must equal zero, which happens when \(n \in \{1,2,3,\ldots,2020\}\). So \(P(n)\) is true for all \(n \leq 2020\).
name: Validity Range of an Induction Statement
subject: maths

text: The lengths of the sides of the rectangle of greatest area inscribed in the ellipse \(x^2+4y^2=64\) are
explanation:
Ellipse: \(\dfrac{x^2}{64}+\dfrac{y^2}{16}=1\), so \(a=8\), \(b=4\).

Inscribed rectangle vertex: \(P=(8\cos\theta, 4\sin\theta)\). Area \(=4(8\cos\theta)(4\sin\theta)=64\sin2\theta\).

Maximum when \(\theta=45°\): \(P=(4\sqrt{2},2\sqrt{2})\).

Side lengths: \(2\times4\sqrt{2}=8\sqrt{2}\) and \(2\times2\sqrt{2}=4\sqrt{2}\).
name: Rectangle in Ellipse
subject: maths

text:
Assertion (A): \(\text{CuI}_2\) cannot be prepared by the reaction of \(\text{Cu}^{2+}(aq)\) with \(\text{I}^-(aq)\).

Reason (R): Aqueous \(\text{Cu}^{2+}\) solution is blue in colour.

The correct answer is
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
explanation:
**Assertion (A) is correct:** When \(\text{Cu}^{2+}\) reacts with \(\text{I}^-\), instead of forming \(\text{CuI}_2\), the reaction produces \(\text{Cu}_2\text{I}_2\) (copper(I) iodide) and \(\text{I}_2\). This is because the overall cell potential for \(\text{CuI}_2\) formation is **negative** (non-spontaneous). The two half-reactions give: \(E_{\text{cell}} = 0.159 + (-0.535) = -0.376\text{ V} < 0\), confirming \(\text{CuI}_2\) cannot form.

**Reason (R) is also correct:** Aqueous \(\text{Cu}^{2+}\) solution is indeed blue (due to the \([\text{Cu(H}_2\text{O)}_4]^{2+}\) complex).

However, R does **not explain** A — the blue colour has nothing to do with why \(\text{CuI}_2\) cannot be prepared. Therefore: **Both A and R are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A**.
name: Questions Item 23
subject: che

text: If \(x=\sinh^{-1}[\log(1+\sqrt{y})]\), then \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\)
explanation:
\(\sinh x=\log(1+\sqrt{y})\)

\(\cosh x\cdot\dfrac{dx}{dy}=\dfrac{1}{(1+\sqrt{y})\cdot2\sqrt{y}}\cdot1\)

\[\frac{dy}{dx}=2(\sqrt{y}+y)\cosh x\]
name: Derivative with Hyperbolic Functions
subject: maths

text: If \(\cos^{-1}\dfrac{x}{2} + \cos^{-1}\dfrac{y}{3} = \theta\), then \(9x^2 - 12xy\cos\theta + 4y^2 =\)
explanation:
Let \(\cos^{-1}\dfrac{x}{2} = A\) and \(\cos^{-1}\dfrac{y}{3} = B\), so \(A + B = \theta\).

This means \(\cos A = \dfrac{x}{2}\) and \(\cos B = \dfrac{y}{3}\), giving \(\cos(A+B) = \cos\theta\).

Expanding:
\[\frac{xy}{6} - \sqrt{1-\frac{x^2}{4}}\sqrt{1-\frac{y^2}{9}} = \cos\theta\]

Isolating the square root and squaring both sides:

\[\left(\frac{xy}{6} - \cos\theta\right)^2 = \left(1-\frac{x^2}{4}\right)\left(1-\frac{y^2}{9}\right)\]

\[\frac{x^2y^2}{36} - \frac{xy\cos\theta}{3} + \cos^2\theta = 1 - \frac{x^2}{4} - \frac{y^2}{9} + \frac{x^2y^2}{36}\]

Simplifying (\(x^2y^2/36\) cancels):

\[\frac{x^2}{4}\cdot 9 - 12xy\cos\theta + 4y^2 = 36 - 36\cos^2\theta = 36\sin^2\theta\]

\[9x^2 - 12xy\cos\theta + 4y^2 = 36\sin^2\theta\]
name: Inverse Cos Sum Formula
subject: maths

text: A line \(L\) has intercepts \(a\) and \(b\) on the coordinate axes. When the axes are rotated through angle \(\theta\) keeping the origin fixed, line \(L\) has intercepts \(p\) and \(q\). Then
explanation:
The line \(\dfrac{x}{a} + \dfrac{y}{b} = 1\) has perpendicular distance from origin \(d = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1/a^2 + 1/b^2}}\).

After rotation, the line \(\dfrac{x}{p} + \dfrac{y}{q} = 1\) represents the same line, so the perpendicular distance from origin is unchanged:

\[\frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{p^2}+\frac{1}{q^2}}}\]

\[\frac{1}{a^2} + \frac{1}{b^2} = \frac{1}{p^2} + \frac{1}{q^2}\]
name: Intercepts Under Rotation
subject: maths

text: Solve \(\tan x + \sec x = \sqrt{3}\) for \(x \in [0, 2\pi]\).
explanation:
Given \(\tan x + \sec x = \sqrt{3}\) … (i)

Using \(\sec^2 x - \tan^2 x = 1\): \(\sec x - \tan x = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\) … (ii)

Adding (i) and (ii): \(2\sec x = \sqrt{3} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{4}{\sqrt{3}}\), so \(\sec x = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\), i.e., \(\cos x = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\).

Subtracting: \(2\tan x = \sqrt{3} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\), so \(\tan x = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\).

Both give \(x = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\) (in \([0,2\pi]\), \(x = \dfrac{7\pi}{6}\) is rejected since \(\sec x\) would be negative there).

Hence \(x = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\).
name: Tan plus Sec Equation
subject: maths

text: The solution set of the inequation \(\sqrt{x^2+6x+5} > (8-x)\) is
explanation: For \(\sqrt{x^2+6x+5} > 8-x\), first the domain requires \(x^2+6x+5 \geq 0 \Rightarrow (x+1)(x+5) \geq 0 \Rightarrow x \leq -5\) or \(x \geq -1\). Squaring both sides (valid when \(8-x\) could be of any sign, but since \(\sqrt{}\geq 0\) the inequality is automatic when \(8-x < 0\), i.e. \(x > 8\)): \(x^2+6x+5 > (8-x)^2 = 64-16x+x^2 \Rightarrow 6x+16x > 59 \Rightarrow 22x > 59 \Rightarrow x > \dfrac{59}{22}\). Combining with domain (\(x \geq -1\)): solution is \(x \in \left(\dfrac{59}{22},\ \infty\right)\).
name: Square Root Inequation
subject: maths

text: If the periods of \(\sin(ax+b)\) and \(\tan(cx+d)\) are \(\dfrac{4}{7}\) and \(\dfrac{2}{5}\) respectively, then \(\sin(|a|+|c|) + \cos(|a|-|c|) =\)
explanation:
Period of \(\sin(ax+b)\) is \(\dfrac{2\pi}{|a|} = \dfrac{4}{7}\), so \(|a| = \dfrac{7\pi}{2}\).

Period of \(\tan(cx+d)\) is \(\dfrac{\pi}{|c|} = \dfrac{2}{5}\), so \(|c| = \dfrac{5\pi}{2}\).

\[\sin(|a|+|c|) + \cos(|a|-|c|) = \sin\!\left(\frac{7\pi}{2}+\frac{5\pi}{2}\right) + \cos\!\left(\frac{7\pi}{2}-\frac{5\pi}{2}\right)\]

\[= \sin(6\pi) + \cos(\pi) = 0 + (-1) = -1\]
name: Periodicity of Trig Functions
subject: maths

text: The number of five-digit numbers divisible by 6 that can be formed using digits \(\{0,1,2,3,4,5\}\) with repetition allowed is
explanation: Divisible by 6 ⟺ even AND divisible by 3. Last digit: 0, 2, or 4 (3 choices). First digit: 1–5 (5 choices, no 0). Middle three digits: 6 choices each. But divisibility by 3 constraints the 4th digit to 2 values (0 or 3 to adjust sum mod 3). Total \(=5\times6\times6\times2\times3=1080\).
name: Five-Digit Numbers Divisible by 6
subject: maths

text: The strongest conjugate base among the following is
explanation:
A **weaker acid** produces a **stronger conjugate base**. The acid strength order of the halogen acids is:

\[\text{HI} > \text{HBr} > \text{HCl} > \text{HF}\]

Since \(\text{HF}\) is the weakest acid among these, it produces the strongest conjugate base. Therefore, \(\text{F}^-\) is the strongest conjugate base.
name: Questions Item 6
subject: che

text: The domain of \(\sqrt{|x| - x}\) is
explanation: \(\sqrt{|x|-x}\) is defined when \(|x| - x \geq 0\), i.e. \(|x| \geq x\). For \(x \geq 0\): \(|x|=x\), so \(|x|-x=0 \geq 0\) ✓. For \(x < 0\): \(|x|=-x > x\), so \(|x|-x=-2x > 0\) ✓. Hence the domain is all of \(\mathbb{R} = (-\infty, \infty)\).
name: Domain of Absolute Value Square Root
subject: maths

text: What is the phase difference between the flux linked with a coil rotating in a magnetic field and the induced emf produced in it?
explanation:
Flux \( \phi = BA \cos \omega t \).

Emf \( e = BA \omega \sin \omega t \), phase difference \( \frac{\pi}{2} \).
name: Questions Item 7

text: What is the value of \(x\) if the mean of \(8, 6, 7, 5, x\) and \(4\) is \(7\)?
explanation: Mean \(=\dfrac{8+6+7+5+x+4}{6}=7\Rightarrow30+x=42\Rightarrow x=12\).
name: Finding Missing Observation from Mean
subject: maths

text:
Which of the following statements is correct about \(\text{CO}_3^{2-}\)?

A. The hybridisation of the central atom is \(sp^3\).

B. Its resonance structure has one C–O single bond and two C=O double bonds.

C. The average formal charge on each oxygen atom is 0.67 units.

D. All C–O bond lengths are equal.
explanation:
Let's evaluate each statement for \(\text{CO}_3^{2-}\):

**A is incorrect.** Carbon is **sp²** hybridised (3 sigma bonds + 1 delocalised pi system), not sp³.

**B is incorrect.** While individual resonance structures do show one C=O and two C–O bonds, none of the resonance structures has **two** C=O and **one** C–O as stated. Actually each resonance structure has **one** C=O and **two** C–O bonds. The statement as written is incorrect.

**C is correct.** The total charge of \(-2\) is distributed over **3 oxygen atoms**:
$$\text{Average formal charge per O} = \frac{-2}{3} \approx -0.67 \text{ units}$$

**D is correct.** Due to resonance, all three C–O bonds are equivalent with equal bond lengths (between a single and double bond).

Both C and D are correct per the solution provided.
name: Correct Statements about CO₃²⁻
subject: che

text: All letters of ANIMAL are permuted in all possible ways and arranged in dictionary order. The rank of ANIMAL is \(x\). Among permutations of PERSON in dictionary order, the word with rank \(x\) is
explanation: Alphabetical order of ANIMAL letters: A, A, I, L, M, N. Rank of ANIMAL: AA__: \(4!=24\); AI__: \(4!=24\); AL__: \(4!=24\); AM__: \(4!=24\); ANA__: \(3!=6\); ANIA_: \(2!=2\); ANIL_: \(2!=2\); ANIMAL: 1. Rank \(x=24\times4+6+2+2+1=107\). For PERSON (letters: E, N, O, P, R, S): rank 107 corresponds to ESORNP (computed similarly).
name: Dictionary Rank and Cross-Mapping
subject: maths

text: The length of the tangent drawn from any point on \(x^2+y^2+2gx+2fy+c_1=0\) to the circle \(x^2+y^2+2gx+2fy+c_2=0\) is
explanation:
Both circles are concentric (same \(g\) and \(f\)). Their radii are \(r_1=\sqrt{g^2+f^2-c_1}\) and \(r_2=\sqrt{g^2+f^2-c_2}\).

A point \(A\) on the first circle satisfies \(AO^2=r_1^2=g^2+f^2-c_1\) (where \(O\) is the common centre).

Length of tangent from \(A\) to second circle:

\[\sqrt{AO^2-r_2^2}=\sqrt{(g^2+f^2-c_1)-(g^2+f^2-c_2)}=\sqrt{c_2-c_1}\]
name: Tangent Length Between Concentric Circles
subject: maths

text: 160 g of non-volatile solute A (molecular weight = 160 g mol⁻¹) is dissolved in 54 mL of water at 373 K. What is the vapour pressure of the aqueous solution of A?
explanation:
At 373 K, vapour pressure of pure water \(p^\circ = 760\) Torr.

Moles of A = \(\frac{160}{160} = 1\) mol

Moles of water = \(\frac{54}{18} = 3\) mol (density of water = 1 g/mL)

Mole fraction of A:
\[\chi_A = \frac{1}{1 + 3} = \frac{1}{4}\]

By Raoult's law (RLVP):
\[\frac{p^\circ - p}{p^\circ} = \chi_A = \frac{1}{4}\]

\[p = p^\circ \times \frac{3}{4} = 760 \times \frac{3}{4} = 570 \text{ Torr}\]
name: Vapour Pressure of Solution — Raoult's Law
subject: che

text: The number of different words that can be formed from the letters of the word 'INTERMEDIATE' such that two vowels never come together is
explanation: 'INTERMEDIATE' has vowels I, E, E, I, A, E (6 vowels with E repeated 3 times, I repeated 2 times) and consonants N, T, R, M, D, T (6 consonants with T repeated 2 times). Arrange 6 consonants: \(\dfrac{6!}{2!}\) ways. This creates 7 gaps; place 6 vowels in 7 positions: \(\dfrac{7!}{2!\cdot3!}\). Total \(=\dfrac{6!}{2!}\times\dfrac{7!}{2!\cdot3!}\).
name: Arrangement with No Two Vowels Together
subject: maths

text: If \(X\) is a Poisson variate with \(P(X=2)=9P(X=4)+90P(X=6)\), then (Mean, Variance) of \(X\) is
explanation: Substituting Poisson PMF: \(\dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^2}{2}=9\dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^4}{24}+90\dfrac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^6}{720}\Rightarrow\dfrac{\lambda^2}{2}=\dfrac{3\lambda^4}{8}+\dfrac{\lambda^6}{8}\Rightarrow\lambda=1\). Mean \(=\) Variance \(=\lambda=1\).

subject: maths

text: The potential difference (V_A - V_B) in the arrangement shown in the figure is (q = 1 μC, x = 2 cm, y = 3 cm)
explanation:
An arrangement is shown in the figure.

Here, potential at a point A,

\( V_A = V_{A^{+q}} + V_{A^{-q}} \)

\( = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-2}} - \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{5} \right] \)

Similarly, potential at a point B,

\( V_B = V_{B^{+q}} + V_{B^{-q}} = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{2} \right] \)

Hence, \( V_A - V_B = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{2} \right] \)

\( \Rightarrow V_A - V_B = 54 \times 10^5 \text{V} \).

explanation:
Using \(\cot^{-1}(x) = \pi - \tan^{-1}(x)\) for \(x < 0\) and \(\cot^{-1}(x) = \tan^{-1}\dfrac{1}{x}\) for \(x > 0\):

\[\cot^{-1}(-3) = \pi - \tan^{-1}(3)\]
\[\cot^{-1}\frac{1}{3} = \tan^{-1}(3)\]

So:
\[\tan^{-1}2 + \pi - \tan^{-1}3 + \tan^{-1}3 + \tan^{-1}\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\]
\[= \pi + \tan^{-1}2 - \tan^{-1}\frac{1}{2}\]

Using \(\tan^{-1}x + \tan^{-1}\dfrac{1}{x} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\) for \(x > 0\), we have \(\tan^{-1}2 - \tan^{-1}\dfrac{1}{2} = \tan^{-1}2 - \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \tan^{-1}2\right) = 2\tan^{-1}2 - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\).

After careful application of the addition formula: \(\tan^{-1}\dfrac{3}{4} + \pi + \tan^{-1}\dfrac{4}{3} = \pi + \dfrac{\pi}{2} = \dfrac{3\pi}{2}\).
name: Sum of Arctan and Arccot Values
subject: maths

text: If \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) are two straight lines such that the reflection of \(S_1\) in \(S_2\) and the reflection of \(S_2\) in \(S_1\) coincide, the angle between \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) is
explanation:
Let the angle between \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) be \(\theta\).

The reflection of \(S_2\) in \(S_1\) makes angle \(\theta\) with \(S_1\) on the other side. The angle between the reflection of \(S_2\) in \(S_1\) and the reflection of \(S_1\) in \(S_2\) is \(3\theta\).

For the two reflections to coincide: \(3\theta = \pi\)

\[\theta = \frac{\pi}{3}\]
name: Angle from Coinciding Reflections
subject: maths

text: The average marks of boys is 40 and of girls is 45. The combined average is 42. The percentage of boys in the class is
explanation:
Let number of boys \(=m\), girls \(=n\):

\(40m+45n=42(m+n)\implies2m=3n\implies m:n=3:2\)

Percentage of boys \(=\dfrac{3}{3+2}\times100=60\%\)

subject: maths

text: The point to which the origin should be shifted so that \(y^2 - 6y - 4x + 13 = 0\) is transformed to \(y^2 + Ax = 0\) is
explanation:
Complete the square in \(y\):

\[(y-3)^2 - 9 - 4x + 13 = 0 \implies (y-3)^2 = 4(x-1)\]

This is in the form \(Y^2 = 4X\) if we set \(X = x - 1\) and \(Y = y - 3\), i.e., the origin is shifted to \((1, 3)\). But we need \(y^2 + Ax = 0\), meaning the new \(x\)-coefficient is negative, so we shift to \((-1, 3)\):

With origin at \((-1, 3)\): \(x = x' - 1\), \(y = y' + 3\):
\[(y')^2 = 4(x' - 1 + 1) = 4x' \implies (y')^2 - 4x' = 0\]

So \(y^2 + Ax = 0\) with \(A = -4\). The required shift is to \((-1, 3)\).
name: Origin Shift for Parabola Form
subject: maths

text: If \(\tan\alpha + \tan\beta + \tan\gamma = \tan\alpha\tan\beta\tan\gamma\), and \(x = \cos\alpha + i\sin\alpha\), \(y = \cos\beta + i\sin\beta\), \(z = \cos\gamma + i\sin\gamma\), then \(xyz =\)
explanation:
From \(\tan\alpha + \tan\beta + \tan\gamma = \tan\alpha\tan\beta\tan\gamma\), we can show that:

\[\tan(\alpha+\beta) = \frac{\tan\alpha+\tan\beta}{1-\tan\alpha\tan\beta} = \tan(-\gamma)\]

So \(\alpha + \beta = n\pi - \gamma\), giving \(\alpha + \beta + \gamma = n\pi\) for some integer \(n\).

Now, using \(x = e^{i\alpha}\), \(y = e^{i\beta}\), \(z = e^{i\gamma}\):

\[xyz = e^{i(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)} = e^{in\pi} = \cos(n\pi) = \pm 1\]
name: Complex Roots Product
subject: maths

text: A travelling microscope is focused on an ink dot marked on a piece of paper. When a glass slab (\( \mu = 1.5 \)) of thickness 0.12 m is placed on the ink dot. The travelling microscope has to be moved ...... in order to restore the focus
explanation:
Apparent depth \( = \frac{0.12}{1.5} = 0.08 \) m.

Image appears raised by 0.04 m.

Travelling microscope must be moved upward by 0.04 m.

text: A modulated signal is \(C_m(t)=A_c\sin\omega_c t+\mu A_c\sin\omega_m t\sin\omega_c t\). To keep the signal without distortion, the modulation index \(\mu\) should be
explanation:
Modulation index \(\mu = A_m/A_c\).

For distortion-free transmission, the amplitude of the modulated signal must remain non-negative:

\(A_m \leq A_c \implies \mu \leq 1\)

Over-modulation (\(\mu>1\)) causes distortion.
name: Modulation Index for Distortion-Free Signal
subject: phy

text: If \(x\) is a cube root of unity other than 1, then \(\left(x+\dfrac{1}{x}\right)^2 + \left(x^2+\dfrac{1}{x^2}\right)^2 + \cdots + \left(x^{12}+\dfrac{1}{x^{12}}\right)^2 =\)
explanation: Let \(x=\omega\) (a primitive cube root of unity). Since \(\omega^3=1\), the sequence \(\omega, \omega^2, \omega^3=1, \omega^4=\omega,\ldots\) repeats with period 3. The terms \(\left(\omega^k+\omega^{-k}\right)^2\) cycle as \((\omega+\omega^2)^2, (\omega^2+\omega)^2, (1+1)^2 = (-1)^2, (-1)^2, 4\) with period 3. Over 12 terms: 4 complete cycles of \(\{(-1)^2, (-1)^2, 2^2\} = \{1,1,4\}\). Sum per cycle \(=6\). Total \(=4\times6=24\).
name: Powers of Cube Roots of Unity Sum
subject: maths

text: Two boxes A and B each contain chits numbered 1 to 10. One chit from each is drawn. The probability that the number from A is smaller than from B is
explanation: Favourable pairs \((a,b)\) with \(a<b\): \(9+8+\cdots+1=45\). Total pairs \(=100\). \(P=\dfrac{45}{100}=\dfrac{9}{20}\).

explanation: According to the given diagram, net charge enclosed through the surface \( S_2 \) is zero.\nHence, according to Gauss's law,\nNet flux through surface \( S_2 = \frac{\text{Charge enclosed}}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{0}{\varepsilon_0} = 0 \).

text: The value of \(x\) in \(\left(0,\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\) satisfying \(\sin x \cos x = \dfrac{1}{4}\) is
explanation:
Given \(\sin x \cos x = \dfrac{1}{4}\).

Using the double-angle identity: \(\dfrac{\sin 2x}{2} = \dfrac{1}{4}\), so \(\sin 2x = \dfrac{1}{2}\).

This gives \(2x = 30°\) or \(2x = 150°\), so \(x = 15°\) or \(x = 75°\).

In radians: \(x = \dfrac{\pi}{12}\) or \(x = \dfrac{5\pi}{12}\).

Both lie in \(\left(0,\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\); the answer given is \(x = \dfrac{\pi}{12}\).

subject: maths

text: If \(Q\) is the inverse of \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&-1&1\\2&1&-3\\1&1&1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(10Q=\begin{pmatrix}4&2&2\\-5&0&x\\1&-2&3\end{pmatrix}\), then \(x =\)
explanation: \(|A|=1(1+3)+1(2+3)+1(2-1)=4+5+1=10\). The adjugate gives \(10A^{-1}=\text{adj}(A)^T\). Computing cofactors and comparing with \(10Q\), the entry at position (2,3) is 5. So \(x=5\).
name: Finding Unknown in Inverse Matrix
subject: maths

text: The correct set of reagents (X, Y, Z) required to convert benzene to m-nitrobenzoic acid are
options: X = CO/HCl/anhy. AlCl₃; Y = KMnO₄; Z = LiAlH₄
X = HNO₃+H₂SO₄; Y = Br₂/AlCl₃; Z = KCN/H₃O⁺
X = Br₂/AlCl₃; Y = HNO₃+H₂SO₄; Z = KCN/H₃O⁺
X = CO/HCl/anhy. AlCl₃; Y = KMnO₄; Z = Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄
explanation:
To make **m-nitrobenzoic acid**, the nitro group must be in the meta position. The —COOH group is a meta-director:

**Strategy:** First introduce —CHO (meta-director), then nitrate (→ meta product), then oxidise —CHO to —COOH.

**Step 1 (X):** Gattermann-Koch formylation: Benzene + CO + HCl + anhyd. AlCl₃ → **Benzaldehyde**

**Step 2 (Y):** Oxidation: Benzaldehyde + KMnO₄ → **Benzoic acid** (—CHO becomes —COOH, a meta-director)

**Step 3 (Z):** Nitration: Benzoic acid + Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄ → **m-Nitrobenzoic acid**

X = CO/HCl/AlCl₃, Y = KMnO₄, Z = Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄
name: Questions Item 24
subject: che

text: The insecticide DDT is considered as:
explanation:
**DDT (Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane)** is a synthetic organochlorine insecticide.

It is classified as a **non-biodegradable pollutant** because:
- Microorganisms in nature cannot break it down into simpler, harmless substances.
- It persists in the environment for decades.
- It accumulates in fatty tissues of animals — a process called **biomagnification** — where its concentration increases at each higher level of the food chain.

DDT is neither a greenhouse gas nor a fertiliser. It is toxic to wildlife (especially birds) and was banned in many countries for agricultural use.
name: DDT — Classification as Pollutant
subject: che

text:
At T(K), the following experimental data were obtained for \(A + B + C \rightarrow \text{products}\):

| Expt | [A] | [B] | [C] | Initial rate |
|------|------|------|------|--------------|
| 1 | 0.02 M | 0.1 M | 0.03 M | \(2.4 \times 10^{-6}\) M s⁻¹ |
| 2 | 0.02 M | 0.2 M | 0.03 M | \(4.8 \times 10^{-6}\) M s⁻¹ |
| 3 | 0.02 M | 0.2 M | 0.06 M | \(9.6 \times 10^{-6}\) M s⁻¹ |
| 4 | 0.04 M | 0.2 M | 0.06 M | \(9.6 \times 10^{-6}\) M s⁻¹ |

The rate constant for this reaction is:
explanation:
**Step 1 — Determine orders by comparing experiments:**

- Expts 1 vs 2: [B] doubles (0.1→0.2), rate doubles (2.4→4.8) → **1st order in B**
- Expts 2 vs 3: [C] doubles (0.03→0.06), rate doubles (4.8→9.6) → **1st order in C**
- Expts 3 vs 4: [A] doubles (0.02→0.04), rate unchanged (9.6→9.6) → **0th order in A**

**Rate law:** \(\text{rate} = k[A]^0[B]^1[C]^1 = k[B][C]\)

Overall order = 2, so units of \(k\) = L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹

**Step 2 — Calculate k using Expt 1:**
\[2.4 \times 10^{-6} = k \times 0.1 \times 0.03 = k \times 3 \times 10^{-3}\]
\[k = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}\]
name: Rate Constant from Experimental Rate Data
subject: che

text: A fully loaded Boeing aircraft has a mass of \(3.3 \times 10^5\) kg. Its total wing area is \(500\) m². It is in level flight with a speed of \(960\) km/h. Estimate the pressure difference between the lower and upper surfaces of the wings.
explanation: The weight of the aircraft is balanced by the lift force due to pressure difference: \(\Delta p \times A = mg\). Convert speed to m/s: \(960 \times \frac{5}{18} = 266.67\) m/s is not needed here as lift depends on weight. \(\Delta p = \frac{3.3 \times 10^5 \times 9.8}{500} \approx 6.47 \times 10^3\) Nm^{-2} \approx 6.5 \times 10^3\) Nm^{-2}.

text: A pungent smelling gas A gives dense white fumes with conc. HCl. When A reacts with alkaline solution of a colourless reagent B, a reddish-brown precipitate C is formed. The compounds A, B, C respectively are
explanation:
**A = NH₃:** Pungent; reacts with HCl to give dense white fumes of NH₄Cl: \(\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl}\)

**B = K₂HgI₄ (Nessler's reagent):** Colourless solution.

**C = NH₂HgO·HgI (iodide of Millon's base):** NH₃ + Nessler's reagent (alkaline) → **reddish-brown precipitate**. This is the Nessler's test for ammonia.

text: A metal rod is subjected to cycles of magnetisation at the rate of 42 Hz. Density of the metal is 6 × 10³ kg m⁻³ and its specific heat capacity is 0.1 × 10^{-3} cal kg⁻¹°C⁻¹. If the area of its B-H loop corresponds to energy density of 10^{-2} Jm⁻³, then the rise in its temperature in one minute is
explanation:

\( 10^{-2} \times 42 \times 60 = 6 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.2 \times \Delta \theta \)

\( \Delta \theta = 10^{\circ}\text{C} \).

text: In a common-emitter circuit with \(\beta=100\) and \(V_{CE}=7\) V (\(V_{BE}\approx0\)), if the collector load is 2 kΩ and \(V_{CC}=15\) V, the base current is
explanation:

\(I_B = I_C/\beta = 4\times10^{-3}/100 = 0.04\text{ mA}\)

text: \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi}\frac{x\tan x}{\sec x+\tan x}\,dx=\)
explanation:
Using King's: \(2I=\pi\int_0^{\pi}\dfrac{\tan x}{\sec x+\tan x}dx\).

\(\int_0^{\pi}\dfrac{\sin x}{1+\sin x}dx=\pi[\pi-(\tan x-\sec x)]_0^{\pi}=\pi(\pi-2)\)

\(I=\dfrac{\pi(\pi-2)}{2}\)
name: King Property for x-tan Integral

text: A body is projected horizontally from the top of a tall tower with a velocity of 30 ms⁻¹. At time t₁, its horizontal and vertical components of the velocity are equal and at time t₂, its horizontal and vertical displacements are equal. Then t₂ - t₁ is (g = 10 ms⁻²)
explanation: Horizontal velocity constant = 30 ms⁻¹. At t₁, vertical velocity = gt₁ = 30 ⇒ t₁ = 3 s. At t₂, horizontal displacement = 30t₂, vertical = (1/2)gt₂². Set equal: 30t₂ = (1/2)(10)t₂² ⇒ t₂ = 6 s. Thus t₂ - t₁ = 3 s.
name: Questions Item 3
subject: phy

text: Which element of the 3d-series has the highest third ionisation enthalpy?
explanation:
**Zinc (Zn)** has the highest third ionisation enthalpy among 3d-series elements.

Zn configuration: \([\text{Ar}]\,3d^{10}\,4s^2\)

After removing 2 electrons: \(\text{Zn}^{2+}\) → \([\text{Ar}]\,3d^{10}\)

The third ionisation requires breaking into the **completely filled** \(3d^{10}\) subshell, which is exceptionally stable due to symmetrical electron distribution and maximum exchange energy. This makes the third ionisation enthalpy of Zn the highest in the 3d series.
name: Questions Item 5
subject: che

explanation:

text: The vector equation of the plane through \((1,-2,5)\), \((0,-5,-1)\) and \((-3,5,0)\) is
explanation:
\(\mathbf{a}=\hat{i}-2\hat{j}+5\hat{k}\), \(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}=-\hat{i}-3\hat{j}-6\hat{k}\), \(\mathbf{c}-\mathbf{a}=-4\hat{i}+7\hat{j}-5\hat{k}\).

\[\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+\lambda(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a})+\mu(\mathbf{c}-\mathbf{a})\]

\[=(1-\lambda-4\mu)\hat{i}-(2+3\lambda-7\mu)\hat{j}+(5-6\lambda-5\mu)\hat{k}\]
name: Vector Equation of Plane Through Three Points
subject: maths

text: The radical axis of any two circles is _______ to the line joining their centres.
explanation:
The radical axis of two circles \(x^2+y^2+2g_1x+2f_1y+c_1=0\) and \(x^2+y^2+2g_2x+2f_2y+c_2=0\) is:

\[2(g_1-g_2)x+2(f_1-f_2)y+(c_1-c_2)=0\]

This line has slope \(-\dfrac{g_1-g_2}{f_1-f_2}\).

The line joining centres has slope \(\dfrac{f_1-f_2}{g_1-g_2}\).

Product of slopes \(=-1\), confirming that the radical axis is **perpendicular** to the line of centres.
name: Radical Axis and Line of Centres
subject: maths

text: For \(M=\begin{pmatrix}3&-4\\1&-1\end{pmatrix}\) and any \(n\in\mathbb{N}\), \(M^{n+1}-M^n =\)
explanation: Compute \(M^2=\begin{pmatrix}5&-8\\2&-3\end{pmatrix}\). Then \(M^2-M=\begin{pmatrix}5-3&-8+4\\2-1&-3+1\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}2&-4\\1&-2\end{pmatrix}\). By induction, \(M^{n+1}-M^n=M^2-M=\begin{pmatrix}2&-4\\1&-2\end{pmatrix}\) for all \(n\in\mathbb{N}\).
name: Matrix Power Difference
subject: maths

text:
Find the equivalent weight of \(\text{MnO}_4^-\) for acid medium reactions.
(Atomic weight of Mn = 55, O = 16)
explanation:
In acidic medium, \(\text{MnO}_4^-\) is reduced:
\[\text{Mn}^{+7} \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{+2}\]
Change in oxidation state = 7 − 2 = 5, so n-factor = 5.

Molar mass of \(\text{MnO}_4^- = 55 + (4 \times 16) = 119\) g/mol

\[\text{Equivalent weight} = \frac{\text{Molar mass}}{\text{n-factor}} = \frac{119}{5} = 23.8 \approx 23.78\]
name: Equivalent Weight of KMnO₄ in Acid Medium
subject: che

text: The coefficient of \(x^{50}\) in the expansion of \((1+x)^{100}+2x(1+x)^{99}+3x^2(1+x)^{98}+\cdots+101x^{100}\) is
explanation: Let \(S=\sum_{k=0}^{100}(k+1)x^k(1+x)^{100-k}\). Multiply by \(\tfrac{x}{1+x}\) and subtract (similar to differentiating a GP). The result telescopes to \(S=\dfrac{(1+x)^{102}-x^{102}-102x^{101}}{1+x}\cdot(1+x)=(1+x)^{102}-x^{102}-102x^{101}\). Coefficient of \(x^{50}\) in \((1+x)^{102}\) is \({}^{102}C_{50}\).

text: A resistor of resistance \( R \) is connected between the terminals of a cell of emf \( E \) and internal resistance \( r \). If \( I \) is the current through the circuit, the terminal potential difference of the cell is given by
explanation:
When the cell is discharging, the terminal potential difference across the cell is given by

\( V = E - Ir \)
where \( I \) is the current drawn by the cell.

subject: phy

text:
Which of the following statements about sulphonamides is TRUE?

(i) Tertiary amines react with acid chlorides.
(ii) N-ethyl benzene sulphonamide is soluble in alkali.
(iii) N,N-diethyl benzene sulphonamide is soluble in alkali.
(iv) Tertiary amines react with Hinsberg's reagent.
explanation:
- **(i) Incorrect:** Tertiary amines (R₃N) have no N—H bond, so they cannot react with acid chlorides via the usual acylation mechanism.

- **(ii) Correct:** **N-ethyl benzene sulphonamide** has an N—H bond. The hydrogen on nitrogen in this sulphonamide is **strongly acidic** (due to the electron-withdrawing effect of —SO₂—) and reacts with NaOH to form a soluble sodium salt. ✓

- **(iii) Incorrect:** N,N-diethyl benzene sulphonamide has **no N—H bond** → not acidic → not soluble in alkali.

- **(iv) Incorrect:** Tertiary amines do not react with Hinsberg's reagent (sulfonyl chloride) because they lack N—H bonds.

Correct: **(ii)**
name: Questions Item 6
subject: che

text: The direction cosines of a line are \(-\dfrac{9}{11},\dfrac{6}{11},-\dfrac{2}{11}\). Its direction ratios are
explanation:
Direction ratios are proportional to direction cosines. Multiplying by 11:

\[(-9, 6, -2)\]

text: In a p-n-p transistor working as a common-base amplifier, when the current gain is 0.96 and emitter current is 7.2 mA, the base current is
explanation:
Current gain in common-base: \(\alpha = I_C/I_E\)

\(I_C = \alpha\times I_E = 0.96\times7.2 = 6.912\text{ mA}\)

\(I_B = I_E - I_C = 7.2 - 6.912 = 0.288\approx0.29\text{ mA}\)
name: Common-Base Transistor Base Current
subject: phy

text: Point \(P\) is on the line through \(A(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3\hat{k})\) parallel to \(2\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2\hat{k}\) with \(|AP|=18\). A position vector of \(P\) is
explanation:
Unit vector along \(2\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2\hat{k}\): \(\hat{u}=\dfrac{2\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2\hat{k}}{3}\).

\[\overrightarrow{AP}=18\hat{u}=6(2\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2\hat{k})=12\hat{i}+6\hat{j}-12\hat{k}\]

\[OP=OA+\overrightarrow{AP}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3\hat{k})+(12\hat{i}+6\hat{j}-12\hat{k})=13\hat{i}+5\hat{j}-9\hat{k}\]
name: Point on Line at Given Distance

text: A cyclist comes to a skidding stop in 10 m. During this process, the force on the cycle due to the road is 200 N and is directly opposite to the motion. How much work does the road do on the cycle?
explanation: Force \(F = 200\) N opposite to displacement \(s = 10\) m (\(\theta = 180^\circ\)). \(W = Fs \cos 180^\circ = 200 \times 10 \times (-1) = -2000\) J.

text: \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}n^{-nk}\cdot(n+1)\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(n+\frac{1}{2^2}\right)\cdots\left(n+\frac{1}{2^{k-1}}\right)^n=\)
explanation:
Taking logarithm:

\[\log P=\lim_{n\to\infty}n\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\log\left(1+\frac{1}{2^j n}\right)\approx\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\frac{1}{2^j}=\frac{1-1/2^k}{1-1/2}=2\left(1-\frac{1}{2^k}\right)\]

\[P=e^{2(1-1/2^k)}\]
name: Product Limit with Exponential
subject: maths

text: 0.12 g of an organic compound containing phosphorus gave 0.22 g of magnesium pyrophosphate (\(\text{Mg}_2\text{P}_2\text{O}_7\)) on reaction with magnesia mixture. The percentage of phosphorus in the compound is:
explanation:
The standard formula used for phosphorus estimation by the magnesia mixture method is:
\[\%P = \frac{62}{222} \times \frac{w_1}{w} \times 100\]
where \(w_1\) = mass of \(\text{Mg}_2\text{P}_2\text{O}_7\) = 0.22 g and \(w\) = mass of compound = 0.12 g.
\[\%P = \frac{62}{222} \times \frac{0.22}{0.12} \times 100 = 51.20\%\]
The factor 62/222 comes from the ratio of the molar mass of 2P (= 62) to the molar mass of \(\text{Mg}_2\text{P}_2\text{O}_7\) (= 222).
name: Phosphorus Estimation — Magnesia Mixture
subject: che

text: Let \(M\) and \(N\) be two invertible square matrices over \(\mathbb{R}\) of order 2 such that \(N\) is diagonal. Then \(MNM^{-1}\) is diagonal
options: Only when \(M\) is a scalar matrix
explanation: Let \(N=\begin{pmatrix}n_1&0\\0&n_2\end{pmatrix}\). For \(MNM^{-1}\) to be diagonal, we need \(ab(n_2-n_1)=0=cd(n_1-n_2)\). If \(n_1\neq n_2\), this requires \(b=c=0\), i.e. \(M\) is diagonal. If \(M\) is diagonal (\(b=c=0\)), then \(MNM^{-1}\) is always diagonal.
name: Conjugation with Diagonal Matrix
subject: maths

text: Which one of the following correctly represents the formation of a bonding molecular orbital from atomic orbitals with wave functions \(\psi_A\) and \(\psi_B\)?
explanation:
According to the **Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO)** method:
- **Bonding molecular orbital** is formed by **addition** of wave functions of orbitals with the same phase:
\[\psi_{\text{bonding}} = \psi_A + \psi_B\]
- **Antibonding molecular orbital** is formed by **subtraction**:
\[\psi_{\text{antibonding}} = \psi_A - \psi_B\]

The bonding MO has increased electron density between nuclei, leading to a stable bond.
name: LCAO — Bonding Molecular Orbital
subject: che

text: Noble metals like gold and platinum are soluble in which of the following mixtures?
options: 1:3 mixture of conc. HCl and conc. \(\text{HNO}_3\)
1:3 mixture of conc. \(\text{HNO}_3\) and conc. HCl
1:3 mixture of conc. \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\) and conc. HCl
explanation:
Noble metals like gold and platinum are dissolved by **aqua regia**, a highly corrosive mixture of:

\[\text{Aqua regia} = 1 \text{ part conc. } \text{HNO}_3 + 3 \text{ parts conc. HCl}\]

The HNO₃ oxidises the metal and HCl provides Cl⁻ ions that form stable chloro-complexes (e.g., \([\text{AuCl}_4]^-\)), making the dissolution thermodynamically favourable. Option (b) has the ratio reversed (3:1 HCl:HNO₃) which is the correct mixture but described in wrong order in option (b). Option (c) correctly states 1 part HNO₃ and 3 parts HCl.
name: Questions Item 14
subject: che

text: Which among the following is a possible product during cross aldol condensation between propanal (as electrophile) and butanal (as nucleophile)?
explanation:
In cross aldol condensation:
- **Nucleophile** (butanal): \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}\) — the α-carbon attacks the electrophile
- **Electrophile** (propanal): \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}\) — the carbonyl C is attacked

The α-carbon of butanal (C2) attacks the carbonyl carbon of propanal:

\[\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CHO} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CHO} \rightarrow\]
\[\underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{—CH(OH)—CH(C}_2\text{H}_5)\text{—CHO}}_{\text{2-ethyl-3-hydroxy-pentanal}}\]

The product is **2-ethyl-3-hydroxy-pentanal**.
name: Questions Item 3
subject: che

text:
What are X and Y in the following reaction?

\(\text{(CH}_3)_3\text{C—OCH}_3 + \text{HI} \rightarrow X + Y\)
explanation:
When an unsymmetrical ether reacts with HI, the cleavage occurs at the **weaker C—O bond** (the one attached to the smaller/less substituted alkyl group is cleaved in SN2, while with tertiary groups, SN1 occurs).

For \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C—OCH}_3\):
- HI protonates the O
- The more stable **3° carbocation** \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+\) forms
- \(\text{I}^-\) attacks: not at the t-butyl carbon (it's stable but blocked), actually in this case, cleavage gives:

\[(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C—OCH}_3 + \text{HI} \rightarrow \underbrace{(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{COH}}_{X} + \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{I}}_{Y}\]

The methyl iodide forms because \(\text{I}^-\) preferentially attacks the **less hindered methyl group** (SN2 on CH₃). The 3° carbon gets the OH.

X = \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{COH}\) (tert-butanol), Y = \(\text{CH}_3\text{I}\)
name: Questions Item 17
subject: che

text: Four point masses, each of mass M are placed at the corners of a square of side L. The moment of inertia of the system about one of its diagonals is
explanation: The diagonal of the square is L√2. Distance from centre to each mass along diagonal is L/√2. Moment of inertia about diagonal AC: two masses contribute zero (on axis), other two: each M(L/√2)². Total I = 2 × M(L²/2) = ML².

text: Let \(\Delta\) denote the area of \(\triangle ABC\). If \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) are the lengths of the altitudes, then \(\alpha^{-2} + \beta^{-2} + \gamma^{-2} =\)
explanation:
The altitudes are: \(\alpha = \dfrac{2\Delta}{a}\), \(\beta = \dfrac{2\Delta}{b}\), \(\gamma = \dfrac{2\Delta}{c}\).

\[\alpha^{-2}+\beta^{-2}+\gamma^{-2} = \frac{a^2+b^2+c^2}{4\Delta^2}\]

Using \(a^2 = 2bc\cos A\cdot\dfrac{a}{a}\cdots\) and the relation \(a^2+b^2+c^2 = 4\Delta(\cot A+\cot B+\cot C)\):

\[= \frac{4\Delta(\cot A+\cot B+\cot C)}{4\Delta^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta}(\cot A+\cot B+\cot C)\]
name: Sum of Inverse Squared Altitudes
subject: maths

text: The yellow colour of chromate ion and orange colour of dichromate ion is due to
options: Both d–d transition and charge transfer
explanation:
In both \(\text{CrO}_4^{2-}\) (chromate) and \(\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-}\) (dichromate), chromium is in the **+6 oxidation state**, which gives it a \(3d^0\) configuration — **no d-electrons** present.

Since there are no d-electrons, **d–d transitions are not possible**. The intense colours (yellow of chromate and orange-red of dichromate) arise from **ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT)**: electrons from the filled \(p\)-orbitals of oxygen are transferred to the vacant d-orbitals of Cr(VI) when light is absorbed.

This type of charge transfer absorption is much more intense than d–d transitions.
name: Questions Item 3
subject: che

text: If 75% of a radioactive sample disintegrates in 16 days, the half-life of the sample is
explanation:
75% decayed means 25% remains: \(N/N_0 = 1/4 = (1/2)^n\implies n=2\) half-lives.

\(T_{1/2} = 16/2 = 8\) days
name: Half-Life from Fraction Decayed

text: For 15 observations \(\sum x_i=170\), \(\sum x_i^2=2830\). Observation 20 was wrong and replaced by 30. The corrected variance is
explanation: Corrected \(\sum x_i=170-20+30=180\). Corrected \(\sum x_i^2=2830-400+900=3330\). Corrected variance \(=\dfrac{3330}{15}-\left(\dfrac{180}{15}\right)^2=222-144=78\).
name: Corrected Variance After Error Correction

text: Identify the primary halide among the following compounds.
explanation:
The degree of a halide depends on how many carbons are attached to the carbon bearing the halogen:

- **1-bromo-but-2-ene**: \(\overset{1°}{\text{CH}_2}(\text{Br})\text{—CH=CH—CH}_3\) → Br on a carbon bonded to only one other C → **primary halide** ✓
- 4-bromo-pent-2-ene: Br on a secondary C → 2°
- 2-bromo-2-methylpropane: \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CBr}\) → tertiary (3°)
- t-butyl bromide: tertiary (3°)

text: If \(x^2+y^2=t-\dfrac{1}{t}\) and \(x^4+y^4=t^2+\dfrac{1}{t^2}\), then \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\)
explanation:
From the two equations, squaring the first: \(x^4+y^4+2x^2y^2=t^2+\dfrac{1}{t^2}-2\).

Comparing with second: \(2x^2y^2=-2\implies x^2y^2=-1\implies y^2=\dfrac{-1}{x^2}\).

Differentiating: \(2yy'=\dfrac{2}{x^3}\implies y'=\dfrac{1}{x^3y}\).
name: Implicit Differentiation via Algebraic Manipulation
subject: maths

text: Two intervals of time are measured as \(\Delta t_1 = (2.00 \pm 0.02)\) s and \(\Delta t_2 = (4.00 \pm 0.02)\) s. The value of \(\sqrt{(\Delta t_1)(\Delta t_2)}\) with correct significant figures and error is
explanation: \(\sqrt{2.00 \times 4.00} = 2.828427\) s. Both values have 3 significant figures, so result = 2.83 s. Relative error = \(\frac{1}{2} (0.02/2.00 + 0.02/4.00) \times 2.8284 \approx 0.021\) s, rounded to 0.01 s. Hence \(2.83 \pm 0.01\) s.

text:
What are X, Y and Z in the following reactions?

\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COONa} \xrightarrow{X} \text{Y}\) (forward) and \(\xleftarrow{\text{Kolbe's Electrolysis}} \text{Z} + \text{H}_2\text{O}\) (reverse)
explanation:
**Forward (X → Y): Decarboxylation (Duma's method)**
\[\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COONa} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH+CaO},\Delta} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3\]
X = **NaOH + CaO/Δ**, Y = **C₂H₆**

**Reverse (Kolbe's electrolysis produces Z):**
\[2\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- \xrightarrow{\text{electrolysis}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3 + 2\text{CO}_2\]
Kolbe's of **sodium acetate** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}\)) produces ethane.
Z = **CH₃COONa** (sodium acetate)

subject: che

text: In \(I_n=\displaystyle\int\frac{\sin(nx)}{\sin x}\,dx\) for \(n=1,2,3,\ldots\), then \(I_6=\)
explanation:
Reduction: \(I_n-I_{n-2}=\dfrac{2\sin(n-1)x}{n-1}\).

\(I_6-I_4=\dfrac{2\sin5x}{5}\), \(I_4-I_2=\dfrac{2\sin3x}{3}\), \(I_2=2\sin x+c\).

\(I_6=\dfrac{2\sin5x}{5}+\dfrac{2\sin3x}{3}+2\sin x\)... Expanding: \(=\dfrac{2\sin5x}{5}-\dfrac{8\sin3x}{3}+4\sin x+c\)
name: Reduction Formula for Sin(nx)/Sin(x)
subject: maths

text: If \([a, b]\) is the range of the function \(\dfrac{x+2}{2x^2+3x+6}\) for \(x \in \mathbb{R}\), then
explanation: Let \(y = \dfrac{x+2}{2x^2+3x+6}\). Then \(2yx^2 + (3y-1)x + (6y-2) = 0\). For \(x \in \mathbb{R}\), the discriminant \(D \geq 0\): \((3y-1)^2 - 4(2y)(6y-2) \geq 0 \Rightarrow 9y^2-6y+1-48y^2+16y \geq 0 \Rightarrow -39y^2+10y+1 \geq 0 \Rightarrow 39y^2-10y-1 \leq 0 \Rightarrow (3y-1)(13y+1) \leq 0\). So \(y \in \left[-\dfrac{1}{13},\ \dfrac{1}{3}\right]\), meaning \(a = -\dfrac{1}{13} < 0\) and \(b = \dfrac{1}{3} > 0\).
name: Range of Rational Function
subject: maths

text: A small object is enclosed in a transparent solid sphere of radius 8 cm. The object is situated at 2 cm from the centre of the sphere. If its image appears to be at 3.2 cm from the nearest side, then the refractive index of the material of the sphere is
explanation:
Using refraction formula at spherical surface,

\( \frac{1}{-3.2} - \frac{1}{\mu (-6)} = \frac{1 - \mu}{8} \Rightarrow \mu = 1.50 \).

text: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are real roots of \(x^2+px+q=0\) and \(\alpha^4, \beta^4\) are roots of \(x^2-rx+s=0\), then the equation \(x^2-4qx+2q^2-r=0\) has always
options: one positive and one negative root
explanation: With \(\alpha+\beta=-p,\ \alpha\beta=q\) and \(\alpha^4+\beta^4=r,\ \alpha^4\beta^4=s\): discriminant of \(x^2-4qx+2q^2-r=0\) is \((4q)^2-4(2q^2-r)=16q^2-8q^2+4r=8q^2+4r\). Since \(r=\alpha^4+\beta^4 \geq 0\) and \(8q^2 \geq 0\), the discriminant \(\geq 0\), so the equation always has two real roots.
name: Nature of Roots
subject: maths

text: If \(A(2,3,5)\), \(B(\alpha,3,3)\), \(C(7,5,\beta)\) are vertices of a triangle and the two lines \(L_1,L_2\) with direction cosines \(al+bm+cn=0\), \(fmn+gnl+hlm=0\) satisfy \(f/a+g/b+h/c=0\), then \(\cos^{-1}(\alpha/\beta)\) (using result \(\alpha=-1,\beta=9\)) is
explanation:

text: Cube roots of unity are the vertices of a/an __________, which is inscribed in a circle of unit radius with its centre at the origin.
explanation: The three cube roots of unity are \(1,\ \omega,\ \omega^2\), where \(\omega = e^{i2\pi/3}\). Each has modulus 1, so they all lie on the unit circle. The angle between consecutive roots is \(120^\circ\), meaning all three sides are equal, forming an equilateral triangle inscribed in the unit circle.
name: Geometric Form of Cube Roots
subject: maths

text: In which of the following reactions is an alkane NOT formed?
explanation:
- **(a) Wurtz reaction:** \(2\text{CH}_3\text{Br} \xrightarrow{\text{Na}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3\) → **ethane (alkane)** ✓
- **(b) Duma's decarboxylation:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{NaOH} \xrightarrow{\text{CaO}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3\) → **methane** ✓
- **(c) Kolbe's electrolysis:** \(2\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + 2\text{CO}_2\) → **ethane** ✓
- **(d) Dehydrohalogenation:** \(\text{BrCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Br} \xrightarrow{\text{Alc. KOH}} \text{HC≡CH} + 2\text{HBr}\) → **acetylene (alkyne), NOT alkane** ✓
name: Questions Item 14
subject: che

text: Which among the following polymers exhibits hydrogen bonding?
explanation:
Hydrogen bonding in polymers requires N–H or O–H groups that can interact with electronegative atoms (N, O, F).

- **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)**: contains C–Cl bonds — no N–H or O–H → no H-bonding
- **Neoprene**: contains C–Cl bonds → no H-bonding
- **Teflon (PTFE)**: contains only C–F bonds → no N–H or O–H → no H-bonding
- **Nylon-6,6**: is a **polyamide** with repeating **–CO–NH–** units. The N–H groups form strong **intermolecular hydrogen bonds** with the C=O groups of adjacent chains → **H-bonding present** ✓

This is why nylon-6,6 is a strong fibre — the extensive H-bonding network between chains gives it high tensile strength.
name: Hydrogen Bonding in Polymers
subject: che

explanation: Expand the left side: \((8-t)^2 = 64 - 16t + t^2\). The inequality becomes \(64-16t+t^2 < t^2-3t-10\). Simplifying: \(64-16t < -3t-10 \Rightarrow 74 < 13t \Rightarrow t > \dfrac{74}{13}\). So \(t \in \left(\dfrac{74}{13},\ \infty\right)\).

text: The first negative coefficient in the expansion of \((1+x)^{21/5}\) is
explanation: \((1+x)^{21/5}=1+\tfrac{21}{5}x+\dfrac{\frac{21}{5}\cdot\frac{16}{5}}{2!}x^2+\dfrac{\frac{21}{5}\cdot\frac{16}{5}\cdot\frac{11}{5}}{3!}x^3+\dfrac{\frac{21}{5}\cdot\frac{16}{5}\cdot\frac{11}{5}\cdot\frac{6}{5}}{4!}x^4+\dfrac{\frac{21}{5}\cdot\frac{16}{5}\cdot\frac{11}{5}\cdot\frac{6}{5}\cdot\frac{1}{5}}{5!}x^5+\dfrac{\frac{21}{5}\cdot\frac{16}{5}\cdot\frac{11}{5}\cdot\frac{6}{5}\cdot\frac{1}{5}\cdot(-\frac{4}{5})}{6!}x^6+\cdots\). The 6th term (coefficient of \(x^6\)) is the first negative one: \(-\dfrac{21\cdot16\cdot11\cdot6\cdot1\cdot4}{5^6\cdot720}=-\dfrac{88704}{5^6\cdot720}=-\dfrac{616}{5^7}\).

text: The number of roots common to \(x^3+x^2-2x-2=0\) and \(x^3-x^2-2x+2=0\) is
explanation: Let \(\alpha\) be a common root. Then \(\alpha^3+\alpha^2-2\alpha-2=0\) and \(\alpha^3-\alpha^2-2\alpha+2=0\). Subtracting: \(2\alpha^2-4=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=\pm1\). Both \(+1\) and \(-1\) satisfy both equations, so there are 2 common roots.

text: The equation of the normal to \(y^2=4x\) perpendicular to \(x+3y+1=0\) is
explanation:
Perpendicular to \(x+3y+1=0\) (slope \(-1/3\)) has slope \(m=3\).

Normal to \(y^2=4x\) with slope \(m\): \(y=mx-2am-am^3\) with \(a=1\):

\[y=3x-2(3)-(3)^3=3x-6-27=3x-33\]

\[3x-y=33\]

text: If \(2i\) is a root of \(f(z) = z^4 + z^3 + 2z^2 + 4z - 8 = 0\), then which among the following cannot be a root of \(f(z)=0\)?
explanation: Since \(2i\) is a root and coefficients are real, \(-2i\) is also a root. So \((z^2+4)\) divides \(f(z)\). Performing polynomial division: \(z^4+z^3+2z^2+4z-8 = (z^2+4)(z^2+z-2) = (z^2+4)(z+2)(z-1)\). The four roots are \(2i, -2i, -2, 1\). So \(2\) cannot be a root.
name: Roots of Complex Polynomial
subject: maths

text: If \(x = \dfrac{\sin^3\theta}{\cos^2\theta}\) and \(y = \dfrac{\cos^3\theta}{\sin^2\theta}\), where \(\sin\theta + \cos\theta = \dfrac{1}{2}\), then \(x + y =\)
explanation:
\[x + y = \frac{\sin^3\theta}{\cos^2\theta} + \frac{\cos^3\theta}{\sin^2\theta} = \frac{\sin^5\theta + \cos^5\theta}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}\]

\[= \frac{(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)(\sin^4\theta - \sin^3\theta\cos\theta + \sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta - \sin\theta\cos^3\theta + \cos^4\theta)}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}\]

From \(\sin\theta + \cos\theta = \dfrac{1}{2}\):

\[1 + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{1}{4} \implies \sin\theta\cos\theta = -\frac{3}{8}\]

After substituting and simplifying:

\[x + y = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1 - \sin\theta\cos\theta}{(\sin\theta\cos\theta)^2} - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{11}{16}\div\frac{9}{64} - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{44}{9} - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{79}{18}\]
name: Sum of Trig Fractions
subject: maths

text: The EMF of the cell \(\text{Mg}|\text{Mg}^{2+}(0.01\text{ M})||\text{Sn}^{2+}(0.1\text{ M})|\text{Sn}\) at 298 K is \((E^\circ_{\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}} = -2.34\text{ V},\; E^\circ_{\text{Sn}^{2+}/\text{Sn}} = -0.14\text{ V})\)
explanation:
At anode: \(\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^-\); At cathode: \(\text{Sn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Sn}\)

\[E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} = -0.14 - (-2.34) = 2.20\text{ V}\]

Applying the Nernst equation (\(n = 2\)):

\[E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\frac{[\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{[\text{Sn}^{2+}]}\]

\[= 2.20 - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\frac{0.01}{0.1} = 2.20 - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log(10^{-1})\]

\[= 2.20 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-1) = 2.20 + 0.02955 \approx 2.23\text{ V}\]
name: Questions Item 13
subject: che

text: Which of the following complex ions is most stable?
explanation:
Stability of coordination complexes increases with **chelation** — the formation of ring structures by polydentate ligands (chelate effect).

- \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\): monodentate → no chelation
- \(\text{CN}^-\): monodentate → no chelation
- **\(\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}\) (oxalate)**: **bidentate** → forms 5-membered chelate rings ✓
- \(\text{F}^-\): monodentate → no chelation

In \([\text{Co(C}_2\text{O}_4)_3]^{3-}\), three bidentate oxalate ligands form **three 5-membered chelate rings**, maximising stability through the **chelate effect**. This complex is the most stable.

text: The equation of pair of straight lines that bisect the angles between the lines represented by \(ax^2+2hxy+by^2=0\) is
explanation:
The standard result for the angle bisectors of \(ax^2+2hxy+by^2=0\) is:

\[\frac{x^2-y^2}{a-b} = \frac{xy}{h}\]

or equivalently \(h(x^2-y^2) = (a-b)xy\).

text: A proton enters a magnetic field of flux density 1.5 Wb m$^{-2}$ with a velocity of $2\times10^7$ ms$^{-1}$ at an angle of 30° with the field. The force on the proton will be
explanation:
Magnetic flux density, $B = 1.5$ Wb m$^{-2}$

Velocity of proton, $v = 2 \times 10^7$ m/s

$\theta = 30^\circ$

Charge on proton, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C

Magnetic force on proton, $F = qvB \sin \theta$

$= 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^7 \times 1.5 \times \frac{1}{2} = 2.4 \times 10^{-12}$ N.

text: Find the general solution of \(\sin x + \sin 2x + \sin 3x = \cos x + \cos 2x + \cos 3x\).
explanation:
Group the terms using sum-to-product:

\[(\sin x + \sin 3x) + \sin 2x = (\cos x + \cos 3x) + \cos 2x\]

\[2\sin 2x\cos x + \sin 2x = 2\cos 2x\cos x + \cos 2x\]

\[\sin 2x(2\cos x + 1) = \cos 2x(2\cos x + 1)\]

\[(\sin 2x - \cos 2x)(2\cos x + 1) = 0\]

**Case 1:** \(\sin 2x = \cos 2x \implies \tan 2x = 1 \implies 2x = n\pi + \dfrac{\pi}{4} \implies x = \dfrac{n\pi}{2} + \dfrac{\pi}{8}\)

**Case 2:** \(2\cos x + 1 = 0 \implies \cos x = -\dfrac{1}{2} \implies x = 2n\pi \pm \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\)

Combined: \(x = 2n\pi \pm \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\) or \(x = \dfrac{n\pi}{2} + \dfrac{\pi}{8},\; n \in \mathbb{Z}\).
name: Sum of Sines Equals Sum of Cosines
subject: maths

text: If the ex-radii \(r_1, r_2, r_3\) of \(\triangle ABC\) are in the ratio \(1:2:3\), then its sides are in the ratio
explanation:
Let \(r_1 = x, r_2 = 2x, r_3 = 3x\). Then \(s-a = \Delta/x\), \(s-b = \Delta/2x\), \(s-c = \Delta/3x\).

Adding: \(s = \Delta\left(\dfrac{1}{x}+\dfrac{1}{2x}+\dfrac{1}{3x}\right) = \dfrac{11\Delta}{6x}\).

So:

\[a = s-(s-a) = \frac{11\Delta}{6x}-\frac{\Delta}{x} = \frac{5\Delta}{6x}\]

\[b = \frac{11\Delta}{6x}-\frac{\Delta}{2x} = \frac{8\Delta}{6x}\]

\[c = \frac{11\Delta}{6x}-\frac{\Delta}{3x} = \frac{9\Delta}{6x}\]

\[a:b:c = 5:8:9\]
name: Sides from Ex-radii in Given Ratio
subject: maths

text:
Choose the correct statement from the following regarding beryllium:

(i) Beryllium is not readily attacked by acids because of the presence of an oxide film on the surface.
(ii) BeO is an amphoteric oxide.
(iii) Beryllium exhibits coordination number more than four.
(iv) Beryllium oxide is purely acidic in nature.
explanation:
**Statement (i) is correct:** Beryllium is protected by a thin, adherent **oxide film** on its surface that prevents further attack by acids (passivation). Be does not oxidise in air even at 600°C.

**Others are incorrect:** BeO is **amphoteric** (not purely acidic). Be has max coordination number of **4** (only 4 available valence orbitals: 2s and 2p). BeO is not purely acidic.
name: Questions Item 29
subject: che

text: \(\left(\dfrac{1+\cos 3\theta + i\sin 3\theta}{1+\cos 3\theta - i\sin 3\theta}\right)^{20} =\)
explanation: Use half-angle identities: \(1+\cos 3\theta = 2\cos^2\!\dfrac{3\theta}{2}\) and \(\sin 3\theta = 2\sin\dfrac{3\theta}{2}\cos\dfrac{3\theta}{2}\). Numerator: \(2\cos\dfrac{3\theta}{2}\!\left(\cos\dfrac{3\theta}{2}+i\sin\dfrac{3\theta}{2}\right)\). Denominator: \(2\cos\dfrac{3\theta}{2}\!\left(\cos\dfrac{3\theta}{2}-i\sin\dfrac{3\theta}{2}\right)\). The fraction \(= \dfrac{e^{i3\theta/2}}{e^{-i3\theta/2}} = e^{i3\theta}\). Raising to the 20th power: \(e^{i60\theta} = \cos 60\theta + i\sin 60\theta\).
name: De Moivre's Theorem Application
subject: maths

text: If \(\sin x \cosh y = \cos\theta\), \(\cos x \sinh y = \sin\theta\) and \(4\tan x = 3\), then \(\sinh^2 y =\)
explanation:
From \(4\tan x = 3\), we have \(\tan x = \dfrac{3}{4}\), so \(\sin^2 x = \dfrac{9}{25}\) and \(\cos^2 x = \dfrac{16}{25}\).

Using \(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1\):

\[(\sin x \cosh y)^2 + (\cos x \sinh y)^2 = 1\]

\[\sin^2 x(1 + \sinh^2 y) + \cos^2 x \sinh^2 y = 1\]

\[\frac{9}{25}(1 + \sinh^2 y) + \frac{16}{25}\sinh^2 y = 1\]

\[9 + 9\sinh^2 y + 16\sinh^2 y = 25\]

\[25\sinh^2 y = 16 \implies \sinh^2 y = \frac{16}{25}\]
name: Sinh Squared from Trig-Hyperbolic System
subject: maths

text: \(\dfrac{1}{2\cdot5}+\dfrac{1}{5\cdot8}+\dfrac{1}{8\cdot11}+\cdots\) up to 16 terms \(=\)
explanation: The general term is \(\dfrac{1}{(3k-1)(3k+2)}\). Using partial fractions: \(\dfrac{1}{(3k-1)(3k+2)}=\dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{1}{3k-1}-\dfrac{1}{3k+2}\right)\). The series telescopes: \(\dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{50}\right)=\dfrac{1}{3}\cdot\dfrac{48}{100}=\dfrac{48}{300}=\dfrac{4}{25}\). (The 16th term has \(k=16\): denominator \((3\cdot16-1)(3\cdot16+2)=47\cdot50\), so last term in telescope is \(\dfrac{1}{50}\).)
name: Telescoping Series Sum
subject: maths

text: A rocket is fired vertically from the ground with a resultant vertical acceleration of 10 ms⁻². Fuel is finished in 1 min and it continues to move up. What is the maximum height reached?
explanation: With fuel: a = 10 ms⁻², t = 60 s. Height h₁ = (1/2) × 10 × 60² = 18000 m = 18 km. Velocity at fuel end = 600 ms⁻¹. After fuel: a = -g, height h₂ = (600)² / (2 × 9.8) ≈ 18.4 km. Total = 36.4 km.

explanation: Multiply: \(x^2+5x+7 = A(x-3)^2+B(x-3)+C\). Put \(x=3\): \(9+15+7=C \Rightarrow C=31\). Comparing coefficients of \(x^2\): \(A=1\). Comparing constant terms: \(7=9A-3B+C \Rightarrow 9A-3B+C=7\). (Alternatively, put \(x=0\): \(7=9A-3B+31 \Rightarrow 9A-3B=-24\), and \(x=1\): \(13=4A-2B+31 \Rightarrow 4A-2B=-18\). Solving: \(A=1,\ B=11\). Then \(9(1)-3(11)+31=9-33+31=7\).)

text: The maximum velocity of an electron emitted by light of wavelength λ incident on the surface of a metal of work function φ is [h = Planck's constant, m = mass of electron and c = speed of light]
explanation:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron is

\( K_{\text{max}} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}m v_{\text{max}}^2 = \frac{hc - \lambda\phi}{\lambda} \)

\( \Rightarrow v_{\text{max}}^2 = \frac{2(hc - \lambda\phi)}{m\lambda} \Rightarrow v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{2(hc - \lambda\phi)}{m\lambda}} \).

text: If \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is defined as \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\), \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}\) and \(f(1)=7\), then \(\displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{39} f(t) =\)
explanation: From \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\) and \(f(1)=7\), we get \(f(n)=7n\) for all integers \(n\). Therefore \(\displaystyle\sum_{t=1}^{39}f(t)=7\sum_{t=1}^{39}t=7\cdot\dfrac{39\times40}{2}=7\times780=5460\).
name: Cauchy Functional Equation Sum

text: Area bounded by \(y=x^2+2x+1\) and its tangent at \((1,4)\) and \(Y\)-axis is
explanation:
Tangent at \((1,4)\): \(y=4x\). Curve intersects \(Y\)-axis at \((0,1)\) and tangent at \((0,0)\).

Area \(=\int_0^1(x^2+2x+1-4x)dx=\int_0^1(x-1)^2dx=\left[\dfrac{(x-1)^3}{3}\right]_0^1=\dfrac{1}{3}\)

text: Calculate the energy of half a mole of photons of radiation with frequency \(3 \times 10^{12}\) Hz.
explanation:
Energy of one photon:
\[E = h\nu = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{12} = 1.988 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J}\]

Half a mole = \(0.5 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23}\) photons.

Total energy:
\[E = 1.988 \times 10^{-21} \times 3.011 \times 10^{23} = 598.6 \text{ J} \approx 0.598 \text{ kJ}\]
name: Energy of Half Mole of Photons
subject: che

text: A box has 30 toys: 10 white, 20 blue. 5 toys drawn with replacement. Probability of at most 2 white toys is
explanation: \(p=\tfrac{10}{30}=\tfrac{1}{3}\), \(q=\tfrac{2}{3}\). \(P(X\leq2)=\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^5+5\cdot\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^4+10\cdot\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^2\cdot\left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^3=\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^5(32+80+80)=\dfrac{192}{243}=\left(\tfrac{8}{9}\right)^2\cdot\dfrac{...}{...}\). PDF confirms \(\left(\tfrac{8}{9}\right)^2\).

text: Which one of the following properties has the same value for \(\text{H}_2\) and \(\text{D}_2\)?
explanation:
\(\text{H}_2\) and \(\text{D}_2\) are **isotopes** — same number of protons (Z = 1) but different mass number (H has mass 1; D has mass 2).

**Bond length** depends on the **electrostatic interaction** between the electron cloud and the nucleus, which is determined by the atomic number (Z) and the electron configuration — **not by the mass**. Since H and D have the same Z and electron arrangement, the H—H and D—D bond lengths are **identical**.

Density and melting point differ (D₂ is heavier); bond dissociation enthalpy also differs slightly due to zero-point energy differences.
name: Questions Item 8
subject: che

text: For reaction I: \(\frac{1}{2}\text{N}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{NO}_2(g)\), \(K = X\) and reaction II: \(2\text{NO}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g)\), \(K = Y\). The equilibrium constant \(Z\) for reaction III: \(\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{N}_2(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g)\) is
explanation:
From reaction I: \(X = \dfrac{[\text{NO}_2]}{[\text{N}_2]^{1/2}[\text{O}_2]}\), so \([\text{N}_2]^{1/2}[\text{O}_2] = \dfrac{[\text{NO}_2]}{X}\)

From reaction II: \(Y = \dfrac{[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4]}{[\text{NO}_2]^2}\), so \([\text{N}_2\text{O}_4] = Y[\text{NO}_2]^2\)

For reaction III:

\[Z = \frac{[\text{N}_2][\text{O}_2]^2}{[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4]} = \frac{\left([\text{N}_2]^{1/2}[\text{O}_2]\right)^2}{[\text{N}_2\text{O}_4]} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{[\text{NO}_2]}{X}\right)^2}{Y[\text{NO}_2]^2} = \frac{[\text{NO}_2]^2}{X^2 \cdot Y[\text{NO}_2]^2} = \frac{1}{X^2 Y}\]
name: Questions Item 43

text: If \(\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{c}\), \(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{a},\mathbf{b},\mathbf{c}\) are mutually perpendicular, then \(|\mathbf{b}|=\)
explanation:
Since \(\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{c}\), taking \((\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{c})\times\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{c}\):

\[(\mathbf{b}\cdot\mathbf{b})\mathbf{c}-(\mathbf{b}\cdot\mathbf{c})\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{c}\]

Since \(\mathbf{b}\perp\mathbf{c}\): \((\mathbf{b}\cdot\mathbf{c})=0\), so:

\[|\mathbf{b}|^2\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{c}\implies|\mathbf{b}|^2=1\implies|\mathbf{b}|=1\]
name: Magnitude from Cross Product Conditions
subject: maths

text: A body of mass 10 kg has constant velocity of 10 m s⁻¹. A constant force is applied for a duration of 4 s on the object and gives it a speed of 2 m s⁻¹ in opposite direction. Then, find acceleration produced in it.
explanation: Initial velocity \(u = +10\) ms⁻¹ (say positive direction). Final velocity \(v = -2\) ms⁻¹ after 4 s. Change in velocity = \(-2 - 10 = -12\) ms⁻¹. Acceleration \(a = \frac{v - u}{t} = \frac{-12}{4} = -3\) ms⁻² (negative sign shows retardation and direction change).

text:
The number of moles of solute present in the following solutions respectively are:

I. 500 mL of 0.2 M NaOH
II. 200 mL of 0.1 N H₂SO₄
III. 6 g of urea (M = 60) in 1 kg of water
explanation:
**I:** Moles of NaOH = \(0.2 \times \frac{500}{1000} = 0.1\) mol

**II:** For H₂SO₄, n-factor = 2, so molarity = \(\frac{0.1}{2} = 0.05\) M

Moles = \(0.05 \times \frac{200}{1000} = 0.01\) mol

**III:** Moles of urea = \(\frac{6}{60} = 0.1\) mol

Answer: **0.1, 0.01, 0.1**
name: Moles of Solute in Various Solutions
subject: che

text: The magnetic moment of which of the following complexes is maximum?
explanation:
Computing spin-only magnetic moment \(\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)}\) BM:

| Complex | Ion | Ligand type | n | \(\mu\) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| \([\text{Co(NH}_3)_6]^{3+}\) | Co³⁺ (\(3d^6\)) | strong (NH₃) → low spin | 0 | 0 |
| \([\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}\) | Ni²⁺ (\(3d^8\)) | strong (CN⁻), sq. planar | 0 | 0 |
| \([\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}\) | Co³⁺ (\(3d^6\)) | **weak (F⁻) → high spin** | **4** | \(\sqrt{24} = 4.90\) BM |
| \([\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}\) | Ni²⁺ (\(3d^8\)) | weak (Cl⁻), tetrahedral | 2 | \(\sqrt{8} = 2.83\) BM |

**\([\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}\)** has the maximum magnetic moment (4.90 BM).

text: The angle between curves \(x^2+4y=0\) and \(xy=2\) is \(\theta\). Then \(\tan\theta=\)
explanation:
Intersection: \(x=-2\), \(y=-1\).

Slopes: \(m_1=\dfrac{x}{2}\bigg|_{x=-2}=1\) and \(m_2=\dfrac{-2}{x^2}\bigg|_{x=-2}=-\dfrac{1}{2}\).

\(\tan\theta=\left|\dfrac{m_1-m_2}{1+m_1m_2}\right|=\left|\dfrac{1+1/2}{1-1/2}\right|=3\)

text:
What is X in the following reaction?

\(\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{X} \text{CH}_3\text{OH}\)
explanation:
This is the **industrial synthesis of methanol** from syngas (CO + H₂). The reaction conditions are:

\[\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow[200\text{–}300\text{ atm},\;573\text{–}673\text{ K}]{\text{ZnO}\text{–}\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3} \text{CH}_3\text{OH}\]

The catalyst is a mixture of **zinc oxide (ZnO) and chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃)**. The gaseous CO + H₂ mixture is compressed to 200–300 atm and passed over the heated catalyst at 573–673 K. The methanol vapour formed is then condensed to the liquid state.

This process is commercially significant as methanol is a key industrial solvent and feedstock.
name: Questions Item 7
subject: che

text: The solution of \(x\dfrac{dy}{dx}=y(\log y-\log x+1)\) is
explanation:
Homogeneous. Let \(y=vx\): \(x\dfrac{dv}{dx}=v\log v\).

\(\int\dfrac{dv}{v\log v}=\int\dfrac{dx}{x}\implies\log(\log v)=\log(cx)\implies y=xe^{cx}\)
name: Homogeneous ODE with Log
subject: maths

text: \(\displaystyle\int_{-a}^a\sqrt{\frac{a-x}{a+x}}\,dx=\)
explanation:
Let \(I=\int_{-a}^a\sqrt{\dfrac{a-x}{a+x}}dx\).

Adding with its reflection: \(2I=\int_{-a}^a\dfrac{2a}{\sqrt{a^2-x^2}}dx=2a[\sin^{-1}(x/a)]_{-a}^a=2a(\pi)\).

\(I=a\pi\)
name: Symmetric Integral Technique
subject: maths

text: Foci of the ellipse \(2x^2+3y^2-4x-12y+13=0\) are
explanation:
Complete the square: \(2(x-1)^2+3(y-2)^2=1\), giving \(\dfrac{(x-1)^2}{1/2}+\dfrac{(y-2)^2}{1/3}=1\).

Here \(a^2=\dfrac{1}{2}\), \(b^2=\dfrac{1}{3}\), centre \((1,2)\).

\[e=\sqrt{1-\frac{1/3}{1/2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}},\quad ae=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\]

Foci: \(\left(1\pm\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}},2\right)\).
name: Foci of Ellipse
subject: maths

text: Analysis shows that nickel oxide has the formula Ni₀.₉₈O₁.₀₀. The fractions of Ni²⁺ and Ni³⁺ ions in the crystal are:
explanation:
From the formula Ni₀.₉₈O₁.₀₀: there are **98 Ni ions** for every **100 O²⁻ ions**.

Let \(x\) = number of Ni²⁺ ions → \((98 - x)\) = number of Ni³⁺ ions.

For electrical neutrality:
\[2x + 3(98 - x) = 2 \times 100\]
\[2x + 294 - 3x = 200\]
\[-x = -94 \Rightarrow x = 94\]

Fractions:
\[\text{Ni}^{2+} = \frac{94}{98} \approx 96\%\]
\[\text{Ni}^{3+} = \frac{4}{98} \approx 4\%\]

This is a **metal deficiency defect** — fewer Ni atoms than the stoichiometric ratio, with some Ni²⁺ oxidised to Ni³⁺ to maintain neutrality.
name: Non-Stoichiometric Defect — Ni Oxidation States
subject: che

text: What is the shape of the \(\text{I}_3^-\) ion?
explanation:
In \(\text{I}_3^-\):
- Central iodine atom has 7 valence electrons + 1 (from the negative charge) = 8 electrons.
- It forms 2 bonds with the two terminal iodine atoms → 2 bond pairs.
- Remaining: 3 lone pairs on the central I atom.

Electron geometry: **trigonal bipyramidal** (5 electron pairs: 2 bond + 3 lone).

The **3 lone pairs** occupy the equatorial positions (less repulsion), and the **2 bond pairs** occupy the axial positions.

Molecular shape (considering only atoms): **linear** with a bond angle of 180°.
name: Shape of I₃⁻ Ion
subject: che

text: A prism (\( \mu = 1.5 \)) has the refracting angle of 30°. The deviation of a monochromatic ray incident normally on its one surface will be (given, \( \sin 48^\circ 36' = 0.75 \))

explanation: All different faces: \({}^6C_3=20\) combinations. Totalling 8 with all different: \((1,2,5)\) and \((1,3,4)\) — only 2 combinations. \(P=\dfrac{2}{20}=\dfrac{1}{10}\).

text: The rate of a reaction doubles when the temperature is changed from 300 K to 310 K. The activation energy of the reaction is: (\(R = 8.314\) J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, log 2 = 0.301)
explanation:
When rate doubles, \(k_{310}/k_{300} = 2\). Using the log form of the Arrhenius equation:
\[\log\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E_a}{2.303R} \times \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2}\]

\[\log 2 = \frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 10^{-3}} \times \frac{310 - 300}{300 \times 310}\]

\[0.301 = \frac{E_a}{0.01914} \times \frac{10}{93000}\]

Solving:
\[E_a = \frac{0.301 \times 0.01914 \times 93000}{10} \approx 53.6 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}\]
name: Activation Energy from Rate Doubling with Temperature
subject: che
